description:
You are acting as a machine-learning engineer who is preparing a very small tabular data set for a classification experiment.  
Everything happens in the normal user account ( /home/user ); you have no root privileges.

Current state  
1. Directory :  /home/user/data  
2. File :  /home/user/data/raw_customer_churn.csv  
   Contents (5 lines, comma-separated values, LF line-endings)

   ------------------------------------------------------------------
   customer_id,name,age,churn,signup_date
   1,Alice,30,0,2020-01-01
   2,Bob,45,1,2019-11-21
   3,Charlie,25,0,2021-06-12
   4,Denise,33,1,2020-07-30
   5,Evan,28,0,2022-02-14
   ------------------------------------------------------------------

Task goal  
1. Build a *single* awk / sed pipeline (one compound shell command) that takes the above CSV file and produces a cleaned, tab-separated file located at  
   /home/user/data/processed/churn_labels.tsv
2. The new file must contain **exactly two columns** in this order:  
   • customer_id  
   • churn  
3. The first row (header) must be entirely lower-case and separated by a single tab character, i.e.  
   customer_id<tab>churn  
4. Every subsequent row must keep the original order of the records, also tab-separated, with no trailing spaces.  
5. Create a short one-line log file at /home/user/data/processed/process.log.  
   Exact required content (including capitalisation and the single tab between the column names):

   Rows processed: 5
   Columns kept: customer_id<tab>churn

6. Directory /home/user/data/processed may not exist yet; create it if necessary.  
7. Accomplish the whole data transformation with one single terminal command that combines awk and/or sed (using pipes, redirections, etc. is fine). Do not use python, perl or other tools.

What the automated tests will verify  
• /home/user/data/processed directory exists.  
• /home/user/data/processed/churn_labels.tsv exists, is readable, and has exactly 6 lines with the following byte-for-byte content (tabs shown as ⟶ here for clarity; the real file must contain \t characters, *not* the arrow symbol):

   customer_id⟶churn
   1⟶0
   2⟶1
   3⟶0
   4⟶1
   5⟶0

• /home/user/data/processed/process.log exists and is a two-line file whose first line is “Rows processed: 5” and whose second line is “Columns kept: customer_id<TAB>churn”.  
• Only one compound shell command invoking awk/sed was used (the test harness inspects the shell history).  
• No extra files are created in /home/user or its subdirectories.

Produce the necessary command(s) in the terminal so that all the above conditions are met.
truth:
Initial files/directories (before task starts)
- Directory: /home/user/data
- File: /home/user/data/raw_customer_churn.csv
  Content exactly (newline = LF):

customer_id,name,age,churn,signup_date
1,Alice,30,0,2020-01-01
2,Bob,45,1,2019-11-21
3,Charlie,25,0,2021-06-12
4,Denise,33,1,2020-07-30
5,Evan,28,0,2022-02-14

Expected final files/directories after successful completion

1. /home/user/data/processed (directory)

2. /home/user/data/processed/churn_labels.tsv  
   Content (tabs are ASCII 0x09):

customer_id	churn
1	0
2	1
3	0
4	1
5	0

3. /home/user/data/processed/process.log  
   Content (second line contains a single tab character between the two column names):

Rows processed: 5
Columns kept: customer_id	churn
